market_context: This period was dominated by one macro variable: the 2026 Iran war and its gradual de-escalation. Since early March 2026, the US-Iran conflict had caused Brent crude to surge ~51% m/m on Strait of Hormuz closure fears, triggering stagflation concerns and a risk-off equity regime. By late March, the S&P 500 was near correction territory with University of Michigan consumer sentiment at a multi-year low of 53.3 (March 28). The Q1 quarter-end on March 31 saw a sharp relief rally (+2.9% S&P) on Iran peace signals. April 2 was the one-year anniversary of 'Liberation Day' tariffs (April 2, 2025) — by 2026 the Supreme Court had struck down those tariffs in February 2026, removing a major overhang. April 3 (Good Friday) was a NYSE holiday. The pivotal event arrived April 7-8: Trump announced a two-week US-Iran ceasefire contingent on Strait of Hormuz reopening, triggering one of the largest single-day tech rallies of 2026, with semis (+5%+), Meta (+6.5%), AMZN, GOOGL and NVDA surging. Oil plunged ~15%. April 9 saw follow-through with a negative outlier: Palantir dropped ~7% after Michael Burry warned of rising competition from Anthropic. The dominant trade this period was LONG tech/semis on Iran de-escalation and SHORT energy/defensives. The key alpha source was pre-positioning into the ceasefire catalyst rather than chasing after April 8.

trades.thesis: Iran war has created artificial risk-off suppression of NVDA despite unchanged AI capex fundamentals. Microsoft, Google, Meta all maintained CapEx guidance throughout the conflict. NVDA trades at a discount to intrinsic AI infrastructure value purely on macro fear. Any Iran de-escalation signal is a re-rating catalyst. Q1 quarter-end window dressing adds near-term support.
trades.catalyst: Iran peace talks building (Pakistan PM mediating); Q1 quarter-end institutional rebalancing into NVDA; Warren Buffett publicly stated Apple sold too early, signaling mega-cap tech re-accumulation trend
trades.lesson: Geopolitical risk-off creates asymmetric long opportunities in secular growth names with unchanged fundamentals. AI infrastructure demand is inelastic to Middle East conflict. The entry on Mar 30 (pre-quarter-end) captured both the Q1 rebalancing bounce and the subsequent Iran ceasefire rally.

trades.thesis: Gold benefits from both tails of the Iran scenario: if war continues, stagflation hedge demand persists; if ceasefire, USD weakness as flight-to-safety unwinds slowly while gold remains sticky. Iran war caused one of the largest oil supply disruptions in IEA history, keeping inflation expectations elevated and real rates suppressed — both gold-positive. Position as partial hedge against NVDA long.
trades.catalyst: Iran war fuel crisis driving CPI expectations higher; central bank gold buying trend intact; $US weakening as safe-haven demand shifts
trades.lesson: GLD underperformed thesis because the Iran ceasefire reduced stagflation tail risk faster than anticipated. Gold is a better hedge in early-conflict/escalation phase than late-conflict/resolution phase. Should have sized this as a smaller hedge (1x) vs the NVDA core (3x). Still a valid W given portfolio insurance function.

trades.thesis: XOM is a pure play on the Iran war oil premium. As of March 31, Iran peace signals are multiplying (Pakistan mediating, Iranian president signaling willingness to negotiate). A ceasefire and Strait of Hormuz reopening would collapse the war-risk premium in crude, which accounts for roughly 30-40% of the current oil price level. XOM revenues are highly correlated to crude; consensus estimates do not yet price in ceasefire scenario.
trades.catalyst: Iran President Pezeshkian signaled willingness to end war on March 31; Pakistan PM Sharif brokering ceasefire; Strait of Hormuz reopening would add ~17mbpd back to global supply
trades.lesson: Selling energy into a de-escalation cycle is high-conviction when the oil premium is transparently war-driven. The April 8 ceasefire announcement caused oil to plunge ~15% in a single session. XOM is the cleanest proxy for the oil war-premium with minimal idiosyncratic noise. The 20-day hold captured most of the move, though the stock partially recovered as ceasefire uncertainty remained.

trades.thesis: AMD is the highest-beta beneficiary of an AI infrastructure re-acceleration narrative. Unlike NVDA which carries a premium, AMD trades at a discount with MI300X ramp still early. Q1 2026 earnings cycle will show AI revenue accelerating. Iran ceasefire removes the macro overhang that has suppressed semi valuations. Post-ceasefire, semis historically outperform the broader market by 5-10% in the two weeks following major risk-off resolution. AMD at $210 prices in no ceasefire premium.
trades.catalyst: Q1 earnings season approaching with AMD's MI300X sales acceleration; ASML receiving $16B in orders from SK Hynix and Samsung signals industry capex upcycle; Iran peace trajectory building
trades.lesson: AMD was the single best risk/reward trade of the period. The April 8 ceasefire unlocked a compressed AI re-rating that carried AMD from $210 to $354 in 20 trading days (+68%). The key insight: in a fear-compressed market, high-beta AI semis have the most distance to travel when the macro overhang lifts. Stop was never threatened. Intel also ran 114% in April — the semi sector re-rating was systemic, not stock-specific.

trades.thesis: Nike reported Q3 earnings on March 31 showing a catastrophic miss — stock fell from $52.82 to $44.63 in a single session (-15.5%). The earnings revealed accelerating revenue declines in China, rising tariff costs on Vietnam manufacturing (Vietnam was hit with 46% tariffs in the original Liberation Day package, and while tariffs were struck down in Feb 2026, uncertainty remains), and continued market share loss to emerging competitors. Post-earnings fade continuation likely as sell-side downgrades arrive.
trades.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings miss; China revenue decline acceleration; Vietnam supply chain tariff uncertainty structural headwind; sell-side downgrade cycle typically takes 5-10 trading days post-miss
trades.lesson: NKE did not continue lower as expected — the Iran ceasefire rally created a broad risk-on environment that partially offset fundamental weakness even in structurally impaired names. The thesis was correct (NKE is structurally challenged) but the macro tailwind from de-escalation was too strong. Better entry would have been pre-earnings (Mar 30 at $51) or wait for the risk-on rally to fade. One-day post-earnings entries often lack follow-through as the initial move captures most of the fundamental rerating.

trades.thesis: Dollar General is a defensive name that rallied during the Iran war fear regime as investors sought recession-proof cash flows. With Iran peace signals building, the risk-on rotation will punish defensives as capital flows back to growth. DG also faces structural headwinds: core customer base (low-income consumers) is being squeezed by fuel cost inflation from the war, and any economic normalization post-ceasefire benefits large format retail more than discount dollar stores. DG at $124 prices in continued consumer stress that a ceasefire would partially alleviate — negative either way.
trades.catalyst: Iran war de-escalation risk-on rotation out of defensives; low-income consumer spending patterns stabilizing reduces DG defensive premium; Q1 macro data improving
trades.lesson: DG short worked cleanly — the risk-on regime shift post-ceasefire systematically drained capital from defensives. DG fell from $124 to $104 by May 11, though the 20-day exit captured the first leg. The structural thesis (low-income consumer isn't actually benefiting from a gas-price-driven crisis reversal) was correct: DG faces a Catch-22 where if war ends and gas prices fall, their customer base still faces structural pressures from housing/food inflation.

trades.thesis: META announced the Muse Spark AI platform debut alongside the Iran ceasefire announcement, creating a double catalyst. META is the highest-quality AI monetization story in mega-cap tech with advertising revenue directly tied to AI-enhanced targeting. Iran ceasefire removes the macro overhang that suppressed META's multiple. Digital ad spending was held back during the conflict as CFOs cut discretionary spend; normalization is a 2-quarter tailwind. Muse Spark positions META in generative AI content creation.
trades.catalyst: US-Iran ceasefire announcement April 7-8; Muse Spark AI platform debut; digital advertising recovery from wartime austerity; META Q1 earnings approaching with AI monetization acceleration
trades.lesson: META worked but underperformed AMD and GOOGL from the same period. The Muse Spark launch was well-telegraphed and partly priced in. META carried the April 8 ceasefire rally (+6.5% on the day) but gave back some gains in May as investors rotated to semis for the AI infrastructure story. Should have sized AMD > META in portfolio construction. META is better as a long in the Q2 earnings setup rather than as a ceasefire trade.

trades.thesis: TSMC is the chokepoint of the global AI semiconductor supply chain. As the Iran war suppressed risk appetite, TSMC traded at a discount to its structural value. Two specific catalysts: (1) SK Hynix and Samsung placed ~$16B in combined ASML orders for EUV machines, signaling a new capex upcycle that flows through to TSMC wafer demand; (2) Iran ceasefire removes the geopolitical fear that had particularly weighed on Taiwan-related assets given geographic risk perception. TSMC at $345 prices in prolonged Iran-war regime.
trades.catalyst: ASML $16B order surge from SK Hynix and Samsung signaling AI capex upcycle; Iran ceasefire removes Taiwan geopolitical risk discount; April 17 TSMC earnings approaching with HPC/AI revenue guidance
trades.lesson: TSM was a high-conviction trade with two independent bullish catalysts (capex upcycle + geopolitical de-escalation). The stock hit $402 on April 24 before pulling back to $394 at exit. Staying long through April 24 would have captured +16.5%. The lesson: for fundamental re-rating plays with multiple catalysts, consider extending the hold period or using a trailing stop rather than mechanical 20-day exit.

trades.thesis: Amazon is the largest beneficiary of the Iran war resolution across multiple revenue streams: (1) AWS cloud spending recovers as CFO austerity budgets ease; (2) e-commerce demand normalization as consumer confidence rebounds from war-shock lows; (3) oil price collapse reduces logistics/shipping costs structurally. April 8 ceasefire announcement was the regime-change catalyst. AMZN had underperformed the broader tech rally into the ceasefire, creating a catch-up opportunity.
trades.catalyst: April 8 US-Iran ceasefire; oil price -15% single day reducing AMZN logistics costs; AWS spending recovery as enterprise budgets normalize; consumer confidence recovery from Iran war lows
trades.lesson: AMZN was the cleanest post-ceasefire long: it benefited from the oil cost reduction (logistics), consumer normalization (e-commerce), and enterprise recovery (AWS) simultaneously. The stock went from $221 to $275 in 20 trading days. The April 9 move to $233 (+5.6% day-after) showed momentum. Diversified revenue base made it more resilient than single-product names. Entry on the ceasefire day (not the day after) was critical — waiting would have cost 5.6% immediately.

trades.thesis: Google is a dual beneficiary of the ceasefire: (1) digital advertising recovery as consumer and enterprise confidence normalizes — Google's search advertising is the most correlated asset to consumer economic confidence; (2) Cloud (GCP) spending recovery mirrors AWS. Google also carries no Taiwan geopolitical risk and no supply chain exposure to the Middle East, making it a clean AI play for institutions rotating back into growth. GOOGL at $317 represented a meaningful discount to its fundamental value under normal macro conditions.
trades.catalyst: April 8 Iran ceasefire; digital advertising spending recovery tied to consumer confidence normalization; GCP market share gains in AI inference workloads; Q1 earnings approaching with Search AI Overviews monetization data
trades.lesson: GOOGL was arguably the best quality long of the period — clean balance sheet, no geopolitical exposure, AI monetization accelerating, and advertising recovery directly tied to the ceasefire catalyst. The stock went from $317 to $398 in 20 days (+25%). Entry on the ceasefire day captured the full regime-shift move. GOOGL subsequently hit $400 on May 8. The advertising recovery thesis played out faster than expected as consumer sentiment rebounded sharply on lower gas prices.

trades.thesis: LULU is structurally impaired consumer discretionary facing three compounding headwinds: (1) Iran war has created the largest consumer sentiment collapse since 2008, with UMich sentiment at 53.3 in March — LULU's core customer (affluent urban millennial) is discretionary-sensitive; (2) LULU faces ongoing competitive pressure from Alo, Vuori, and private-label; (3) company is lapping easy comps but gross margin compression is accelerating. The tariff anniversary (April 2) creates renewed supply chain cost anxiety for any company with Asian manufacturing. Consumer discretionary names take 2-4 months to recover even after macro sentiment improves.
trades.catalyst: Iran war consumer confidence at multi-year lows; LULU Q1 earnings approaching with traffic deceleration data; athletic apparel market share erosion to Alo and Vuori; sticky cost inflation in supply chain
trades.lesson: LULU short worked despite the broader risk-on rally because the stock carries idiosyncratic fundamental weakness beyond the macro. The stock fell from $155 to $119 by May 15 (continuing to work after exit). Key insight: high-multiple consumer discretionary stocks with deteriorating fundamentals are not rescued by macro de-escalation — the growth multiple compression continues as fundamental concerns dominate. The ceasefire rally lifted all boats temporarily but LULU's structural issues reasserted within weeks.

period_summary.regime_notes: The March 30 - April 9 period was a classic geopolitical risk-off-to-risk-on regime transition. The dominant playbook: (1) LONG high-quality tech/semis that had been artificially suppressed by Iran war macro fear with unchanged AI fundamentals (NVDA, AMD, GOOGL, AMZN, META, TSM); (2) SHORT energy that priced in sustained war premium (XOM); (3) SHORT consumer discretionary with structural impairment (LULU, DG); (4) LONG gold as a hedge position, sized smaller. The single biggest alpha source was AMD: buying a high-beta semi name at a discount to intrinsic value with a clear geopolitical de-escalation catalyst yielded 68.6% in 20 days. The NKE short was the only FLAT — lesson being that post-earnings entries lack follow-through momentum when the macro environment is net risk-on. The key methodological lesson: in a war-driven risk-off, the premium opportunity is in LONG high-quality tech with inelastic demand (AI infrastructure) — not in SHORT names that already fell, and not in complex cross-asset plays. The April 8 ceasefire announcement was the hinge: regime shifted in a single trading session, and any position entered before April 7 captured the full move.
